nodes.title: Initial Pass
widgets_values: Each total sampler process is arranged in columns thusly. This column contains the default initial sampler and its settings lifted from the default distilled ComfyUI workflow. As it's mostly here to generate (relatively) high-quality audio, and the optional initial video, I advise leaving the settings as they are.

nodes.title: Video Has Audio / Use Video Audio
Video to Video Mode (Disable T2V Mode)
NAG Note
widgets_values: NAG, or Normalized Attention Guidance, is a method which essentially enables negative prompting on models operating at a CFG of 1.0. I've not personally noticed a huge difference while using it, but that may just be a prompting skill issue on my end, so it's here regardless.

nodes.title: Audio to Video Note
widgets_values:
The Audio to Video Mode switch causes the model to use the provided audio as a template. However, during the sampling process, considerable audio quality is lost. Therefore, the Save With Loaded Audio option will overwrite the generated audio with the original audio when the video is saved.

In the Trim Audio Duration node, set the start_index at the point in seconds where you want the audio to begin. The video duration will automatically determine the length of audio used after that point.

nodes.title: T2V Note
widgets_values: Yes, I know it would make more sense to have an I2V switch here, but that would have required a lot more backend nodes to invert boolean outputs, so I beg your indulgence for this idiosyncracy.

blurry, oversaturated, pixelated, low resolution, grainy, distorted, noise, compression artifacts, jpeg artifacts, glitches, watermark, text, logo, signature, copyright, subtitles, distorted sound, saturated sound, loud

nodes.title: Upscaler Pass
widgets_values: This column contains the final refining step and the spatial upscaler with settings again lifted from the default workflow. In my observation, changing any setting here will result in mangled audio, so I tend to leave it alone.

nodes.title: Switches Note
widgets_values:
The Dimension Resize Multipliers refer to how much the initial image/latent is downscaled prior to the initial sampler pass. 1.0 is used when the spatial upscaler is disabled so that the video is generated at the size specified in the Settings column. 0.5 is used when the spatial upscaler is enabled so that the video is generated at half the size, then upscaled in the final pass.

If desired, these values can be changed, particularly the Upscaler one, to increase or reduce the initial resolution of the video. Be advised that the spatial upscaler will ALWAYS upscale by 2x regardless of this value, so adjust according to the limits of your hardware.

nodes.title: Audio Prep Note
widgets_values: The SolidMask node creates a mask over the entire video, based (in this instance) on the audio, which is how audio is synchronized to the video during A2V. Increasing the value setting will REDUCE the impact the audio has on the video and will also permit more of the original audio to be overwritten during sampling. In my experience, this reduces overall quality, so I tend to leave it at 0.

nodes.title: Video to Video Note
widgets_values:
Be sure to disable T2V Mode before running the workflow with V2V Mode enabled or an empty latent will override the provided video.

If your video came with audio that you wish to reuse, enable the Video Has Audio switch. Bear in mind this will also overwrite any audio uploaded via A2V. And it will cause an error if activated when the video has no audio.

Note that I only really use V2V to add audio to old Wan videos, so its functionality is somewhat limited. If you're looking for style switching or more detailed upscaling, a specialized workflow will serve you better.

nodes.title: Settings Note
widgets_values:
Make sure to disable both the Upscaler Mode and Upscaler Node when not needed, unless you want to upscale a fullsize video, in which case leave the latter enabled.

Don't worry too much about LTX's resolution or framerate requirements as there are nodes in place which should calculate the nearest theoretically viable parameters. Though be aware viable does not necessarily mean practical, so maybe avoid massive numbers.

For more granular control over the seeds, simply detach or delete the Seed nodes here and edit the RandomNoise nodes over in the sampler columns.

nodes.title: Finalize Note
widgets_values:
Depending on your hardware, you may find it prudent to replace the VAE Decode node with a VAE Decode (Tiled) node.

The settings of the Tiled node can be left at default except temporal_size which should apparently be increased to 4096, though I have no idea why.

These are just backups of the default ManualSigmas nodes. If desired, use them in place of the ClownScheduler nodes in the Init Pass and Upscaler Pass columns.

You can technically replace all the ClownScheduler nodes if you prefer to specify your own custom sigma curves, but that's rather beyond the scope of this workflow and my own current knowledge.

nodes.title: I2V Note
widgets_values:
When using I2V, the LTXVImgToVideoInplace nodes in all four sampler columns control how closely the model follows the initial image at each sampling stage. The higher the value, the more strongly the initial image affects the first few frames.

If you find your I2V videos freeze for a second at the beginning, lowering these strengths may help.

nodes.title: Save Note
widgets_values: The crf value determines how much your video is compressed during saving. The default for x265 mp4 is 22, which is claimed to be lossless, but I prefer 15. Click on the question mark in the node itself for more comprehensive information.

nodes.title: NAG Settings Note
widgets_values: To be perfectly honest, given I only use the thing half the time anyway, I don't really know what the NAG settings do or whether changing them would result in improved speed or quality. It works well enough as is and there are tutorials out there if you want to learn more.

nodes.title: Image Prep Note
widgets_values:
The Resize Images by Longer Edge node is here as a safety measure to ensure that no video larger than a maximum of 1280x1280 can be generated. This is purely to prevent my computer locking up should I make a mistake in the Settings column. Feel free to alter this according to your own hardware, or delete it outright if you dare.

In my observation, changing the compression in the LTXVPreprocess node doesn't actually do anything unless set to an absurdly high number, but the option is there if needed.

nodes.title: Models Note
widgets_values:
If you are not using GGUF models, replace the Unet Loader with a Diffusion Model Loader and the GGUF DualCLIPLoader with a normal DualCLIPLoader.

If using the Dev model, either up the step count in the samplers to LTX's recommended values or enable the Load LoRA node to activate a distilled LoRA.

My text encoder choice should not be taken as gospel. Though they do produce different outputs, all the Q8 versions of Gemma 3 12b I've tried have been roughly equivalent in quality. I've not tested anything other than Q8s, so can't comment on any other varieties.

nodes.title: Extra Samplers Note
widgets_values:
Generally, all three Extra Samplers switches should match, either true/yes for enabled or false/no for disabled. With all three disabled, this workflow will operate much the same as the default workflow.

However, it is possible to disable just the Low Noise Refiner Pass if desired. This will result in slightly lower quality, but it is faster.

Style: cinematic, fantasy. A handsome, thin young African man with short-cropped hair wearing black sleeveless sorcerer robes walks along a verdant hilltop overlooking a stunning Celtic valley. He is accompanied by an aristocratic, thin European man with flowing white hair wearing elegant sorcerer robes. The African man remarks to the European man in a clear baritone voice with an English accent, "It's quite astonishing, what a little scheduler change can accomplish." As he continues walking, the camera pans up, focusing on the colossal giant spirit tree in the background, overshadowing a distant fantasy city butting up against its roots. The grand orchestral fantasy soundtrack swells to a hopeful crescendo, highlighting the towering majesty of the tree. Ambient sounds include gentle wind rustling through tall grass and distant birdsong. The valley below is filled with rolling hills and sparkling streams, bathed in soft sunlight. The distant city appears ancient and mystical, its towers and spires blending seamlessly with the natural landscape.

nodes.title: Added Passes
widgets_values:
These two columns are the added samplers. The Low Step pass reintroduces creativity while the Refiner Pass helps restore, and sometimes improve upon, visual fidelity. Let me clarify that I claim no expertise here and the settings I'm about to recommend are just what I've been using during testing. They work reasonably well if all you want is to plug in your prompt and go, but I urge experimentation to find your own combination of settings that works best for you.

That out of the way, let's get into it. The settings to be most aware of are located in the ClownScheduler nodes: the scheduler, start_value, scheduler_end_step, and total_steps.

The scheduler determines the sigma values the sampler will utilize to generate the video. In the Low Step pass, linear_quadratic is the default and typically results in the highest fidelity, but just about everything else tends to be more creative. The Refiner pass seems to work best on either linear_quadratic (for greater quality) or bong_tangent (for greater speed), each requiring a different sampler and number of steps. More on that below.

The start_value controls the level of denoising the scheduler employs. The higher the value, the less attention is paid to the initial video. To override the initial video entirely, set to 1.00. This tends to produce the most interesting results, but at arguably the lowest fidelity, depending on the scheduler. LTX seems to be very sensitive to this number during the Low Step pass, so be aware the effect will diminish under about 0.95. The Refiner pass, by contrast, seems to like anywhere from 0.6-0.8 when using linear_quadratic or 0.85 when using bong_tangent.

The scheduler_end_step and total_steps values should always match within their respective ClownSchedulers node. Exceeding 3-4 steps in the Low Step pass will result in the model overcorrecting and returning a video too similar to the default to be worth the extra sampling time. The Refiner pass can vary, with 8 steps working well on linear_quadratic with the euler_ancestral_cfg_pp sampler, or 3 steps on bong_tangent with the euler_cfg_pp sampler (adjusted in the KSamplerSelect node).

Again, there are almost certainly other settings which work as well, if not better, so don't take the defaults I have set up as the final word in quality. One last thing I will expound upon, from the Extra Samplers Note, is that the Refiner pass can be bypassed altogether in order to send the Low Step pass straight to the upscaler. This actually works surprisingly well, and it's obviously faster, but it does seem to lose the most dynamism out of any of the methods, so exercise your own discretion.
